metadata.summary: Run controlled experiments comparing two UI versions on live users to determine which produces better outcomes on a pre-registered metric, with statistical and practical significance checks.

content.title: A/B Testing
content.summary: A/B testing is a controlled experiment that compares two versions of a UI element (control A vs. variant B) on a live user population to determine which produces a better outcome on a defined metric. Design decisions based on opinions create unresolvable debates. A/B testing replaces opinion with behavioral evidence from real users at scale. The hypothesis, primary metric, and minimum sample size must be pre-registered before launch to prevent peeking and moving goalposts.

why.p: Design decisions based on opinions create unresolvable debates. A/B testing replaces opinion with behavioral evidence from real users at scale. It answers "which version performs better" but not "why" — that requires qualitative methods like user interviews.

list.item: A design change has a clear, measurable primary metric (conversion rate, click-through, completion).
Traffic is sufficient for the required sample size (typically 1,000+ conversions/month).
The change is isolated — one variable at a time to enable attribution.
Iterative optimization of an existing flow, not early-stage discovery.
Traffic under ~1,000 conversions/month — sample size makes results meaningless.
Major redesigns with multiple simultaneous changes — too many confounds to attribute results.
When you need to understand why users behave differently — use user interviews instead.
Early-stage product where the primary metric itself is unclear.
Regulatory or safety-critical features where split exposure carries risk.

rule.statement: Pre-register the hypothesis, primary metric, and minimum sample size before launching. Hypothesis format: "If we [change], then [metric] will [improve/decrease] by [amount] because [reason]."
rule.rationale: Pre-registration prevents peeking and moving goalposts; post-hoc hypothesis adjustment inflates false-positive risk. Registered hypotheses also create a historical record of which bets paid off.

example.title: Good hypothesis
example.code: If we change the CTA from "Submit" to "Get Started Free", then click-through rate will increase by 10% because the new label communicates value rather than obligation.

example.title: Bad hypothesis (no baseline)
example.code: We think the button should be larger.
example.note: No metric, no baseline, no expected effect size.

rule.statement: Define three metric types: Primary metric (main outcome being measured), Secondary metrics (related outcomes to monitor), Guardrail metrics (metrics that must not worsen). Check guardrail metrics before declaring a winner, even when the primary metric improves.
rule.rationale: A variant can win on click-through but lose on revenue or error rate; guardrails prevent shipping a net-negative change.

list.item: Primary: checkout completion rate, click-through rate, form submission rate, sign-up rate.
Secondary: time on page, scroll depth, pages per session, feature adoption.
Guardrail: revenue per session, error rate, page load time, bounce rate.

rule.statement: Calculate required sample size before launch using baseline conversion rate, desired minimum detectable effect (MDE), statistical power (80% standard), and significance level (95% standard). Do not end the test early because results look favorable.
rule.rationale: Stopping early on a positive trend inflates false-positive rate (peeking); the full pre-calculated sample is required for valid inference. Sample size depends on baseline; low baselines require larger samples.

rule.statement: Run tests for at least one full business cycle (minimum two weeks) to capture weekday/weekend variation in traffic and conversion patterns.
rule.rationale: Traffic and conversion patterns vary by day of week; a shorter window produces biased samples that do not represent typical user behavior.

rule.statement: Require assessment of both statistical significance (p < 0.05) and practical significance (effect size in business terms). A statistically significant result with tiny effect size (e.g., +0.01% conversion) may not justify implementation cost.
rule.rationale: Practical significance filters out results that are mathematically significant but business-irrelevant. Both tests must pass before shipping.

rule.statement: Compute segment results only for pre-specified segments, not post-hoc exploration. Post-hoc segment analysis inflates false-positive risk via multiple comparisons.
rule.rationale: Pre-specification of segments prevents "fishing" — selectively reporting segments where the variant wins while ignoring segments where it loses.

rule.statement: Apply decision matrix: Clear winner (statistically and practically significant) → implement variant. No difference (not significant) → keep control. Inconclusive → extend test or re-test. Negative impact on guardrail → stop, investigate root cause.
rule.rationale: Structured decision rules prevent motivated reasoning. When there is no clear winner, default to keeping the control — it is already tested in production; the variant adds complexity without demonstrated benefit.

list.item: Clear winner: Both p < 0.05 and effect size justify cost → implement variant.
No winner: Not significant or effect size too small → keep control (simpler is better).
Inconclusive: Extended test, re-test with adjusted parameters, or hold decision pending more data.
Guardrail broken: Variant improves primary metric but worsens guardrail → stop, investigate, do not ship.

antipattern.description: Stopping the test early when results look favorable.
antipattern.reason: Peeking invalidates standard p-values; the full pre-calculated sample is required for valid inference.

antipattern.description: Testing too many things simultaneously in one variant.
antipattern.reason: Multiple simultaneous changes make attribution impossible; you cannot know which change drove the result. Test one variable at a time.

antipattern.description: Ignoring guardrail metrics when declaring a winner.
antipattern.reason: A variant can improve the primary metric while degrading revenue, session depth, or user trust; guardrails prevent shipping net-negative changes.
Undocumented null results cause teams to run the same tests repeatedly; every result has learning value.

antipattern.description: Detecting segment-level wins without checking population-level significance.
antipattern.reason: One segment winning while the full population is flat is not a ship signal; population-level significance is the primary gate. Segment analysis is secondary confirmation.

rule.statement: Agent "ship" recommendations must be confirmed by a PM or analyst before implementation. Agent analysis is advisory; ship decisions require human judgment on business context, timing, and risk appetite.
rule.rationale: Agents can analyze data but cannot assess organization-specific trade-offs or risk tolerance.

rule.statement: Agent cannot detect test contamination (users switching between devices, variants persisting across sessions) in the data; flag this risk in the test plan for engineering to address at tracking layer.
rule.rationale: Cross-device users who see both control and variant pollute assignment integrity; contamination is a data quality issue agents cannot spot from results tables alone.

rule.statement: Agent may misinterpret segment uplift as a universal win. Always check whether the primary metric is significant across the full population before shipping.
rule.rationale: One segment winning while the full population is flat is not a ship signal.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A Claude subagent takes a design change description, baseline metric, and desired improvement threshold and outputs a complete A/B Test Plan document (hypothesis, metrics, guardrails, sample size, duration estimate, risk flags). After the test runs, a second agent pass receives the results table and returns a structured analysis: statistical significance assessment, segment breakdown, guardrail check, and a clear ship/hold/extend recommendation.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — produce the test plan as a structured SDD artifact.
General Claude subagent with analyst role — analyze results CSV and produce the decision report.

section.name: prompt-pattern
section.p: You are a CRO analyst. Given: Change: [describe variant vs. control]; Primary metric: [metric name, current baseline %]; Minimum detectable effect: [%]; Statistical power: 80%, significance level: 95%. Output: 1. Hypothesis statement (If/Then/Because format); 2. Required sample size per variant (show calculation); 3. Estimated test duration at [N] daily visitors; 4. Three guardrail metrics to monitor; 5. Risks and confounds to watch for.

You are a CRO analyst reviewing A/B test results. Input: [results table with control/variant metrics, confidence intervals, p-values, segment breakdown]. Output: 1. Was statistical significance reached? (yes/no, confidence level); 2. Practical significance: is the lift meaningful for the business?; 3. Segment analysis: does the effect hold across key segments?; 4. Guardrail metrics: did any worsen?; 5. Recommendation: Ship / Hold / Extend test — with rationale; 6. Key learning for future tests.

list.item: scipy (Python): Statistical significance calculations, t-tests, chi-squared. pip install scipy. https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/stats.html
statsmodels (Python): Sample size calculation (statsmodels.stats.power). pip install statsmodels. https://www.statsmodels.org
R (base stats): Full frequentist and Bayesian A/B analysis. R -e "prop.test(c(conv_a, conv_b), c(n_a, n_b))"
abba (Node.js): CLI A/B test significance calculator. npm install -g abba. https://github.com/thumbtack/abba
Optimizely (SaaS): Results, experiments, and audiences accessible via REST; agent can pull result data programmatically. Yes (REST API).
VWO (SaaS): Similar to Optimizely; report export via API. Yes (REST API).
Google Analytics 4 (SaaS): A/B test results via GA4 experiments exported through Data API. Yes (Data API).
PostHog (OSS/SaaS): Open-source experimentation with full API access; self-hostable. Yes (REST API).
GrowthBook (OSS/SaaS): Open-source A/B testing platform; agent-friendly REST API for feature flags and results. Yes (REST API).
Statsig (SaaS): Experimentation platform with Bayesian and frequentist modes; REST API for results. Yes (REST API).
Evan Miller's Sample Size Calculator: Reference for manual calculations; use scipy/statsmodels for programmatic equivalent. No API.
Always pre-register the hypothesis, primary metric, and minimum sample size before launching — prevents peeking and moving goalposts.
Run tests for full business cycles (minimum 2 weeks) to capture weekday/weekend variation.
Test one thing at a time in the variant; multiple simultaneous changes make attribution impossible.
Compute segment results only for pre-specified segments — post-hoc segment fishing inflates false-positive risk.
When there is no clear winner, default to keeping the control — simpler is better, and a null result is still a valid learning.
Document every test result, including null results, in a shared experiment log — prevents re-running the same tests.
Agent cannot run the test — it can only plan, analyze data provided to it, and report.
Sample size calculations are sensitive to baseline conversion rate; agent output is only as accurate as the baseline you provide.
Agent analysis of segment data without statistical significance per segment leads to false conclusions (multiple comparisons problem).
Novelty effect: agent cannot detect whether an uplift is short-term curiosity or durable improvement — human judgment on test duration is required.
Agent has no access to your testing platform (Optimizely, VWO, GA4 experiments) without manual data export.
Agent cannot detect test contamination (users switching between control and variant devices) in the data — flag this risk in the test plan.
Agent significance analysis using just "p < 0.05" is fragile; require agent to also assess practical significance (effect size in business terms) and confidence intervals.
Sequential testing (peeking) invalidates standard p-values; if results are checked mid-test, agent should note this caveat explicitly.

template.purpose: A/B test plan: hypothesis, metrics, sample size, timeline, risks.
Results template: metrics table, statistical details, segment analysis, decision.
Python script for sample size calculation and chi-squared significance test.
